coredata.dc:title: Elimination of defect and strain by functionalized CQDs dual-engineering for all-inorganic HTMs-free perovskite solar cells with an ultrahigh voltage of 1.651 V

coredata.dc:description: The high-quality perovskite film with reduced defect states and released lattice strain is crucial to the performance development of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Herein, carbon quantum dots with bromine functional groups (Br-CQDs) are firstly synthesized and creatively employed as additive and buried interface modifier to enhance perovskite film quality. Arising from the strong anchor binding of bromine and oxygen-containing groups in Br-CQDs with uncoordinated Pb2+ and Cs+ ions within perovskite and the insertion of Br-CQDs into perovskite lattice, the incorporation of Br-CQDs exerting the role of an “adhesive” can not only effectively retard nucleation and growth processes, heal defect states and release lattice strain of perovskite film, but also regulate interfaces energy level. As a result, the charge recombination and energy loss inside PSCs are evidently suppressed thanks to the resultant high-quality perovskite film and more matching interfaces energy level. Finally, the hole-free PSC achieves an exciting PCE up to 10.79% with an ultrahigh V OC of 1.651 V, which is to date the highest efficiency and V OC for the mesoporous all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs without hole transport materials. Furthermore, the optimized PSCs display a superior long-term stability under air conditions with 85% RH at 85 °C and continuous illumination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The photographs of different concentrations of Br-CQDs/absolute ethanol solution (left to right: 0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50 mg mL−1) and CQDs/absolute ethanol solution (0.10 mg mL−1) without (top) and with ultraviolet light irradiation (bottom). (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s and Br 3d of Br-CQDs. Top-view SEM images of various (c) PbBr2 and (d) PVK films. (e) XRD patterns, (f) Cs 3d, Pb 4 f, Br 3d and O 1 s XPS spectra of various PVK films. (g) The dark J-V curves of hole-only device with a configuration of FTO/NiO/various PVK/Carbon.

(a) Crystalline grain growth schematic diagram of CsPbBr3 PVK with Br-CQDs modifier. (b, e) AFM, (c, f) KPFM images and (d, g) the corresponding KPFM line scans of the pristine and Br-CQDs tailored PVK films. (h) UV–vis absorption spectrogram of various PVK films. (i) Steady-state PL and (j) TRPL spectra of various PVK films deposited on glass substrate.

(a-c) The typical cross-sectional SEM and (d-f) cross-sectional confocal PL images of various samples. GIXRD patterns of (100) plane for the PVK films (g) without and (h) with Br-CQDs modification. (i) d-spacing values acquired from the GIXRD patterns as a function of the incidence angle. (j) Schematic diagrams of the tensile strain state in PVK films.

(a) Energy level diagram of charge transfer process in Br-CQDs tailored PSCs. (b) The J-V curves, (c) EQE spectra, (d) V OC dependence on light intensity for various PSCs. (e) PL spectra and (f) corresponding TRPL decay spectra of various PVK films deposited on TiO2 substrate. (g) Nyquist plots, (h) Mott-Schottky plots and (i) the dark J-V curves of various CsPbBr3 PSCs.

Normalized PCE of various PSCs without encapsulation as a function of preservative time in air under (a) 85% RH at 85 °C and (b) continuous illumination at maximum power points. (c) The water droplet contact angle of CsPbBr3, CsPbBr3 +Br-CQDs and Br-CQDs/CsPbBr3 +Br-CQDs films.
Photovoltaic data of various PSCs under one sun illumination.

head.ce:title: Elimination of defect and strain by functionalized CQDs dual-engineering for all-inorganic HTMs-free perovskite solar cells with an ultrahigh voltage of 1.651 V

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high-quality perovskite film with reduced defect states and released lattice strain is crucial to the performance development of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Herein, carbon quantum dots with bromine functional groups (Br-CQDs) are firstly synthesized and creatively employed as additive and buried interface modifier to enhance perovskite film quality. Arising from the strong anchor binding of bromine and oxygen-containing groups in Br-CQDs with uncoordinated Pb2+ and Cs+ ions within perovskite and the insertion of Br-CQDs into perovskite lattice, the incorporation of Br-CQDs exerting the role of an “adhesive” can not only effectively retard nucleation and growth processes, heal defect states and release lattice strain of perovskite film, but also regulate interfaces energy level. As a result, the charge recombination and energy loss inside PSCs are evidently suppressed thanks to the resultant high-quality perovskite film and more matching interfaces energy level. Finally, the hole-free PSC achieves an exciting PCE up to 10.79% with an ultrahigh V OC of 1.651 V, which is to date the highest efficiency and V OC for the mesoporous all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs without hole transport materials. Furthermore, the optimized PSCs display a superior long-term stability under air conditions with 85% RH at 85 °C and continuous illumination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Arising from the anchor of bromine and oxygen-containing groups in Br-CQDs with uncoordinated Pb2+ and Cs+ ions at surface and in bulk of PVK and the insertion of Br-CQDs additive in PVK lattice, the introduction of Br-CQDs like a lattice “adhesive” reduces the nucleation and growth, the trap states emergence and the tensile strain of CsPbBr3 films, which helps the HTM-free CsPbBr3 PSCs achieve a champion PCE up to 10.79% with an ultrahigh V OC of 1.651 V and excellent stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thin-film photovoltaic (PV) device, as one of the most promising and cost-effective techniques for directly converting solar energy into electricity, has attracted wide attention from researchers. In the last decade, perovskite (PVK) solar cells (PSCs) have occupied the forefront position of thin-film PV development owing to their simple preparation process and outstanding optoelectronic characteristics of PVK materials including adjustable bandgap range, excellent spectral response, long photo-carrier lifetime and diffusion length, etc. [1–5]. Particularly the certified power conversion efficiency (PCE) of organic-inorganic hybrid PSCs has exceeded 25.7%, which is enough to be comparable to commercial silicon-based solar cells [6]. However, the organic-inorganic PVK halide structure frequently suffers from severe decomposition under the erosion of unfavorable factors such as high humidity, high heat, oxygen and long-term sunlight radiation, etc [7,8]. As a result, the long-term stability of corresponding organic-inorganic hybrid PSCs without any encapsulation is difficult to meet the demands of actual production, which is the biggest obstacle restricting commercial development. Therefore, how to extend the operating stability and service life of PSCs has become the most urgent research topic.

Recently, numerous studies have demonstrated that the preparation of all-inorganic CsPbBrxI3−x (x = 0, 1, 2 or 3) PVKs using inorganic cerium ions (Cs+) instead of organic FA+ and/or MA+ ions can effectively improve the thermal stability of the PSCs without encapsulation [9,10]. Especially, the full-brominated CsPbBr3 PVK exhibits the most superior thermal-, moisture- and UV light-tolerance and a significant potential for application in semitransparent and tandem solar cells because of their inherently high bandgap (2.3 eV), which has been considered as one of the most promising photosensitive materials for thin-film PV. Furthermore, the CsPbBr3 photoactive layer has been widely employed in all-inorganic PSCs free of hole transport materials (HTMs) with a typical configuration of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CsPbBr3/Carbon [8,11–13]. Excitingly, the excellent long-term operation stability, low-cost and simplified structure via simultaneous replacement of precious metals and expensive HTMs with carbon electrode provide a major opportunity to promote the comprehensive commercial development of PSCs [8,12]. However, the initially prepared above HTMs-free device only achieved a champion PCE of 6.7%, which is much lower than the efficiency of the state-of-the-art hybrid PSCs, indicating that boosting the PCE output has become the most important problem to be solved for all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs without HTMs [8]. Recently, researchers have found that the serious non-radiative recombination caused by excessive defect states within PVK film and/or substantial energy loss at PVK/charge transport layers (CTLs) interface, in addition to the intrinsic high bandgap of CsPbBr3, are the culprits for the lower efficiency of devices [11–13].

According to the classical crystallization kinetics, a promising method that introducing multifunctional additives into the PVK precursor solution can effectively prepare more comparable high-quality PVK film with fewer defect states by simultaneously regulating the crystallization process of PVK grain and passivating uncoordinated ions defects. Therefore, several types of additive materials including polymers [14,15], inorganic salts [16,17], organic molecules [18,19] and ionic liquids [20,21] have been investigated to modulate PVK bulk film quality and passivate defects, therefore obviously improving the photoelectric conversion performance of PSCs. However, the unsatisfactory behaviors at TiO2/PVK buried interface, such as residual ions defect and tensile strain as well as large energy barrier, hinder efficient extraction of electron and thereby causing serious energy loss. Therefore, it is yet rather pressing to improve buried interface characteristic while enhancing the PVK bulk quality. Currently, the commonly used buried interface modifiers are mainly organics and organic/inorganic salts, such as n-hexyltrimethyl ammonium bromide and chlorobenzenesulfonic potassium salts etc. [22,23], which can effectively passivate the defects at the underlying surface of PVK film for enhanced PVK quality and devices performance. However, owing to the non-electric conductivity of most organics and salts, it will result in an increase in resistance and trap states for slugglish electrons transfer and more charge recombination when the deposition amount at the interface is slightly miscontrolled. Moreover, considering the less favorable chemical stability of organics and salts at the annealing temperature of the cubic CsPbBr3 phase (about 250 °C), they may not be the most ideal modifiers for improving TiO2/PVK buried interfacial properties in CsPbBr3 PSCs.

Recently, as a carbon-based zero-dimensional material with excellent optical and electrical properties, the suitable optical band gap, environmental friendliness, low-cost and controllable size [24], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have caught the attentions of researchers and been incorporated into PVK or interface to improve the efficiency output and long-term stability of PSCs [25,26]. Unfortunately, these studies are lack of more in-depth research, and the prepared CQDs were usually monotonically used as additives or intermediate energy level materials, which cannot maximize the elimination of undesirable factors inside the device and limits the performance enhancement. In addition, the passivation effect of CQDs toward ion defects of PVK film is minimal owing to the limited amount of oxygen-containing groups. Fortunately, it has been demonstrated that the halogenation of passivation chemicals can efficiently enhance the passivation effect thanks to the passivation function of halogen itself and the adjusted electron cloud density of passivation chemicals [27,28]. Furthermore, the chemical modification of low-dimensional carbon materials with halogen elements has been shown to be able to regulate their band structure, optical, electronic and chemical properties for better application in the area of photoelectrochemistry [29–31]. So, it's an exciting challenge and important mission to synthesize the functional CQDs as the PVK additive and buried interface modifier to simultaneously enhance PVK film quality and TiO2/PVK interface behavior for high performance CsPbBr3 PSCs free of HTMs.

Inspired by the phenomenon, in this work, the multifunctional brominated carbon quantum dots (Br-CQDs) with higher healing effect and electronic conductivity are successfully synthesized and innovatively employed in PSCs. Benefiting from the strong anchor binding of bromine and oxygen-containing groups in Br-CQDs with uncoordinated Pb2+ and Cs+ ions in PVK, Br-CQDs can be employed as additive and buried interface modifier like an “adhesive” to improve the PVK film crystallization kinetics via delaying the crystallization process of CsPbBr3 grains, heal numerous trap states in bulk and at surface of PVK film and release lattice strain in PVK film, regulate interface energy level as well. Consequently, the serious charge recombination and energy loss inside Br-CQDs tailored PSCs are effectively mitigated owing to the more matching interfaces energy level and higher quality perovskite film with enlarged grain size, reduced defect states density, higher electron-holes mobility and released lattice strain. Finally, the PSC with an architecture of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/Br-CQDs/CsPbBr3 +Br-CQDs/Carbon achieves an exciting PCE up to 10.79% with a short-circuit current density (J SC) of 7.84 mA cm−2, an open-circuit voltage (V OC) of 1.651 V and a fill factor (FF) of 83.36%, which is the highest reported PCE and V OC for the mesoporous all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs without any HTMs. Meanwhile, the optimized PSC without encapsulation displays an outstanding long-term stability under 85% RH at 85 °C and continuous illumination in air. Our work provides a promising and effective method of additive and interface dual-engineering to fabricate efficient and stable HTMs-free, all-inorganic PSCs.

Unless otherwise specified, all reagents and materials in this work were obtained from Aladdin and were used directly as received without any purification. TiO2 colloid was synthesized on the basis of our previous work [12]. Carbon paste was purchased from Shanghai Mater Win New Materials Co., Ltd.

Br-CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Firstly, CQDs were synthesized according to the following processes: under constant stirring, 8 g of glucose was mixed in 100 mL of deionized water and heated at 200 °C for 8 min to completely dissolve the glucose. The above solution was repeatedly dialyzed in a 3500D-dialysis membrane for 24 h to obtain a uniform CQDs solution, and then the above solution was freeze-dried for 48 h and fully ground to obtain the CQDs powder. Subsequently, 1 g of CQDs powder and 40 mL of hydrobromic acid was mixed in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and hydrothermally processed at 240 °C for 5 h. Next, the hydrothermal product was directly separated with nylon membrane and respectively washed with deionized water and methanol. Finally, Br-CQDs powder was successfully synthesized after vacuum drying at 40 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of all-inorganic perovskite solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass was etched by 2.0 M HCl and zinc powders to form the desired pattern, the etched glass was respectively rinsed with deionized water and absolute ethanol under ultrasonic conditions. The ethanol solution of ethylene glycol amine (0.5 M) and titanium isopropoxide (0.5 M) was deposited on the FTO surface (7000 rmp, 30 s), then the compact TiO2, c-TiO2, film was obtained after annealing at 500 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the synthesized TiO2 paste was spin-coated on the FTO/c-TiO2 surface (2000 rmp, 30 s) and annealed at 450 °C for 30 min to obtain the mesoscopic TiO2, m-TiO2, layer. Next, the above FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2 substrate was immersed in the TiCl4 aqueous solution at 80 °C for 0.5 h, finally annealed in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for another 0.5 h after successively rinsing by water and ethanol. All the PVK films in this work were fabricated with multi-step solution-processed technique in the completely air atmosphere. Firstly, 90 μL of PbBr2 in DMF solution (1 M) containing 0.10 mg mL−1 of CQDs and different concentration of Br-CQDs (0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25 and 0.50 mg mL−1) were respectively deposited onto the FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2 surface (2000 rpm, 30 s) and thereafter annealing at 90 °C for 20 min for completely evaporating DMF. Afterwards, 90 μL of CsBr in methanol solution (0.07 M) was deposited on the PbBr2 film (2000 rpm, 30 s) and heated at 250 °C on a hot plate for 6 min, the high-quality CsPbBr3 PVK film was successfully produced via repeating the above deposition process of CsBr on the Cs-Pb-Br film surface for seven times. Finally, the conductive carbon slurry was knife-coated on PVK film surface with a control active area of 0.09 cm2 by a template and dried at 90 °C for 15 min for assembling the all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs. In addition, the assembly process of Br-CQDs interface modified PSC was similar with that of control device except for the depositing of Br-CQDs solution onto FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2 substrate (2000 rpm, 30 s).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of various samples were performed on a PHI-5000 C ESCA system (Perkin Elmer, upgraded by RBD) with MgKα radiation (hν =1253.6 eV). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of Br-CQDs were observed by a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping images of various samples were characterized using a field emission SEM (FESEM, S4800, Hitachi). The statistical distribution of grain size for corresponding PVK film was measured from the top-view SEM image by a software named “Nano Measurer”. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of Br-CQDs, Br-CQDs+PbBr2 and Br-CQDs+CsBr samples were detected with a commercial FTIR system (Cary 680, Agilent). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and the depth-dependent grazing incident X-ray diffraction (GIXRD) of different PVK films were measured on an X-ray diffraction (PHILIPSPW1800 diffractometer) with Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Meipuda UV-3200 spectrophotometer was employed to record the ultravioletvisible spectroscopy (UV–vis) absorption spectra of PVK films and Br-CQDs films. Ultraviolet photoemission spectroscopy (UPS) of various PVK films and Br-CQDs films were characterized by the Kratos AXIS ULTRA system using a concentric hemisphere analyzer. The surface potentials of PVK films were tested by Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) with atomic force microscope (AFM, Asylum Co. MFP-3D-SA-DV-OQ). The cross-sectional confocal PL mapping images at 405 nm excitation wavelength were performed by the confocal fluorescence microscope (ISS Q2 FLIM/FFS, ISS Inc.). All the photocurrent-voltage, J-V, curves were performed with a CHI660E electrochemical workstation under simulated AM 1.5 G solar irradiation at 100 mW cm−2 (one sun). The light intensity was calibrated using a solar simulator (SS-F5–3A, Enli Tech) with a silicon reference cell (SRC-00205, Enli Tech). Monochromatic incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectrums of PSCs were recorded at room temperature with a power source (Newport 300 W xenon lamp, 66920). The trap states density of PVK films, the hole mobility for the electron-free devices and the electron mobility of hole-free cells were characterized in the dark with the space-charge limited current (SCLC) method from 0 to 7 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested from 1 MHz to 0.1 Hz in the dark. The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and the corresponding time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectrum of various samples were performed on a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (excited by 385 nm laser) and a Horiba spectrometer (excited at 500 nm laser), respectively. The J-V plots in the dark were recorded from − 1.5–1.5 V at 10 mV s−1 scan rate. The capacitance-voltage (C-V) measurement was carried out in the dark at frequency of 5 kHz with AC amplitude of 30 mV against different bias voltages.

In this work, Br-CQDs were successfully prepared by a hydrothermal method and the details are summarized in the “Experimental” section. As the photographs shown in Fig. 1a, Br-CQDs and CQDs are uniformly dispersed in anhydrous ethanol solution without agglomerative precipitation, the CQDs solution shows bright yellow, and the Br-CQDs solution gradually changes from light yellow to dark black with the increase of Br-CQDs concentration. Moreover, the Br-CQDs and CQDs solutions, upon ultraviolet light illumination, present emission color in comparison with the individual ethanol solvent, which proves the successful preparation of Br-CQDs and CQDs. The color difference between Br-CQDs and CQDs solution without and with ultraviolet light irradiation is mainly ascribed to the effective incorporation and uniform distribution of Br element in CQDs. XPS spectrums are recorded to analyze the elemental compositions and the detailed electronic valence of Br-CQDs. From Fig. S1, the appearance of Br 3d characteristic peak at 68.41 eV in addition to the C 1 s (284.8 eV) and O 1 s (532.8 eV) peaks demonstrates the successful bromination of CQDs. Furthermore, the high-resolution C 1 s, Br 3d and O 1 s XPS are described in Fig. 1b to explore the bromination and oxygen group type of CQDs. Apparently, the C 1 s XPS spectrum is deconvoluted into four characteristic peaks at around 284.60, 285.67, 288.04 and 289.11 eV, relating to the C-C/CC, C-O/C-Br, CO and O-CO bond, respectively. Notably, the C-O and C-Br have the similar binding energy, therefore the single peak at around 285.67 eV belongs to these two bonds [32]. Apart from the physical combination of Br-Br bond at 68.41 eV, for the Br 3d XPS spectrum, the appearance of C-Br bond at 69.56 eV further cross-validates the successful synthesis of Br-CQDs. The O 1 s region exhibits three peaks at 531.77, 532.33 and 536.07 eV, due to CO, C-O-C and O-CO species, respectively, which is in accordance with the results of C 1 s spectra and cross-checks that CQDs contain multiple oxygen groups. The presence of Br and oxygen groups in Br-CQDs can give it the efficient passivation function toward uncoordinated ion defects of PVK film. Moreover, compared to the original CQDs, the bromination of CQDs can enhance its electronic conductivity and passivation effect due to the increase in the free electrons concentration caused by the lower electronegativity of Br [32], therefore contributing to an accelerated transport and separation of carriers in the corresponding PVK film for higher PCE and J SC output. The microstructures of Br-CQDs were also characterized by TEM and shown in Fig. S2. The Br-CQDs with an average diameter of 3.58 nm and an interlayer crystal spacing of 0.23 nm are evenly dispersed in the absolute ethanol solvent, which further verifies the quantum dot structure of Br-CQDs.

Br-CQDs is firstly introduced into precursor solution as additive to prepare high-quality PVK bulk film (A Br-CQDs), and then employed at the TiO2/PVK buried interface to further improve PVK film quality (A/I Br-CQDs) and interface behaviour. As shown in Fig. 1c, the Br-CQDs added PbBr2 film displays a larger porosity than the control one, interestingly, after further depositing Br-CQDs at the TiO2/PVK buried interface, the A/I Br-CQDs tailored PbBr2 film exhibits more voids. The more nanoholes would be conducive to afford sufficient diffusion paths for subsequent CsBr solution to completely bond with PbBr2 and a matching pre-expansion volume of CsPbBr3 PVK grain growth, helping obtain the higher quality PVK films (Fig. 1d) with obviously reduced grain boundary density and larger grain size (Fig. S3). In addition, as the EDS mapping images of A/I Br-CQDs tailored PVK film shown in Fig. S4, all the elements display a uniform distribution free of phase separation, demonstrating the homogeneous addition and existence of Br-CQDs in the PVK films.

To investigate the potential impact of Br-CQDs on the PVK film properties more comprehensively, the crystal structure and trap states density (N t) of various PVK films were measured. According to Fig. 1e, the diffraction peaks intensity of PVK films with Br-CQDs modifier, especially A/I Br-CQDs tailored sample, is remarkably enhanced in comparison with that of the control one, suggesting a higher crystallinity of the former films, which is in consistent with the reduced grain boundaries, larger grain size (Fig. 1d) and lower trap states density (Fig. 1g) of modified PVKs. Interestingly, with the incorporation of Br-CQDs, a slight shift of the diffraction peaks to lower angles is observed, which implies an expansion of corresponding PVK crystalline lattice [33], illustrating that Br-CQDs are inserted into the interstices of the CsPbBr3 lattice rather than the lattice sites of the PVK because of the large radius (1.79 nm) of Br-CQDs (Fig. S2) compared to that of ions in CsPbBr3 PVK. This lattice structure and additives-rich environment help to retard the nucleation and growth process of CsPbBr3 grains to form higher-quality PVK film with higher crystallinity as well as larger grain size [33]. As the optical photographs of various PVK films at different stages displayed in Fig. S5, the Br-CQDs incorporated sample exhibits a much lighter color compared with the control sample before the final formation of PVK, which directly demonstrates the delay of nucleation and growth process of CsPbBr3 after Br-CQDs incorporation. Furthermore, the excess additives situated around the formed PVK crystal and the Br-CQDs layer deposited at buried interface can heal trap states to further enhance CsPbBr3 film quality.

To explore the combination of Br-CQDs with CsPbBr3 and therefore the passivation healing function toward PVK film trap states, the XPS spectrums of different PVK samples were detected and recorded in Fig. 1f. Apparently, all the characteristic peaks of A/I Br-CQDs tailored film compared to those of the other samples display a monotonic shift to lower binding energy. However, the Cs 3d and Pb 4 f peaks display a stronger shift extent towards lower energy than Br 3d, which is mainly ascribed to the combine of bromine and oxygen-containing groups in Br-CQDs with the uncoordinated Cs+, Pb2+ cations of PVK [13]. In addition, the more obvious shift of characteristic peaks for A/I Br-CQDs tailored film compared to that for A Br-CQDs added film also demonstrates the more effective passivation healing effect of A/I Br-CQDs due to further elimination of defects at buried interface. As the FTIR spectra shown in Fig. S6, the slight shift of C-Br, C-O-C, CO and -OH vibration peaks for the pure Br-CQDs film to the lower wavenumber after respectively incorporation with PbBr2 and CsBr strongly verifies the XPS analysis. Moreover, the O 1 s characteristic peak can be evidently observed at 532.18 eV in the modified samples, further indicating that Br-CQDs has successfully maintained inside the formed PVK film. To intuitively evaluate the reduced trap states in the modified PVK films, the J-V curves for the various hole-only devices were measured according to SCLC method in the dark and presented in Fig. 1g. The N t value can be determined in term of the trap-filled limit voltage (V TFL) through the following formula: V TFL = N t qL 2/2εε0 [34]. From Table S1, the N t value significantly reduces from 4.18 × 1015 cm−3 for the control film and 2.27 × 1015 cm−3 for A Br-CQDs incorporated film to 2.06 × 1015 cm−3 for A/I Br-CQDs tailored PVK film, which demonstrates that Br-CQDs efficiently heal PVK defect states, highly agreeing well with XPS analysis.

Based on the above obtained analysis results, it is proposed a mechanism of effectively retarded growth processes and healed defect states of PVK film, as outlined in Fig. 2a. Owing to the strong anchor binding of bromine and oxygen-containing groups in Br-CQDs with uncoordinated Pb2+ and Cs+ ions in PVK, the Br-CQDs layer at buried interface and the partial Br-CQDs inserted into the interstices of the PVK lattice can improve the PVK crystallization kinetics via retarding the nucleation and growth process of CsPbBr3 grain, the accumulation of excess Br-CQDs at the grain boundaries and at the buried interface can also heal numerous trap states including grain boundaries and uncoordinated ions defects to achieve the higher-quality film with higher crystallinity, larger grain size and fewer defects, and therefore suppressing the charge recombination for higher PCE and J SC output. To directly identify the grain enlargement and defect healing after introducing Br-CQDs as well as the charge recombination reduction inside the resulting PVK film, the AFM with KPFM was also tested. Compared to the control film in Fig. 2b, the A/I Br-CQDs tailored PVK film (Fig. 2e) appears a larger grain size and a considerably lower roughness with RMS of 25.3 nm, helping to form a close interface contact with the next deposited carbon electrode to advance charge extraction. As the KPFM surface potential mappings and corresponding line scans of various PVK films depicted in Fig. 2c, d and Fig. 2f, g, respectively, the yellow spots distribution of control film is obviously more discontinuous and surrounded by the numerous purple patterns, indicating that the film displays abundant grain boundaries with higher surface potential than that inside the crystals, which is mainly ascribed to the smaller grain and charged defects enrichment near the grain boundaries. As expected, the Br-CQDs tailored PVK film exhibits a uniform distribution with insignificant purple patterns (Fig. 2f) as well as a smaller surface potential difference (Fig. 2d) than the control film (Fig. 2g), suggesting that a lower surface potential barrier exists between the grain boundaries and CsPbBr3 PVK crystals; that is, a lower energy barrier for accelerating carrier transport and suppressing charge recombination inside the modified film. The reason behind this difference is almost attributed to the enlarged CsPbBr3 grain size and effective defects healing after introducing Br-CQDs, which further validates the above formation mechanism of Br-CQDs tailored film summarized in Fig. 2a.

As presented in Fig. 2h, all Br-CQDs modified samples display a higher absorption peak intensity than the control one, cross-checking the enhanced PVK films quality. The steady-state PL and TRPL spectroscopy of various PVK films directly deposited on glass substrates are shown in Fig. 2i and j to explore the photoluminescence behaviour and fluorescence lifetime inside the PVK films, respectively. From Fig. 2i, the higher PL intensity of modified samples, especially A/I Br-CQDs tailored film, than that of the control one further indicates a significantly suppressed defect-induced non-radiative recombination because of the more effective passivation and the lower grain boundaries in the former films. And the blue shift of PL peak for Br-CQDs tailored samples suggests the indeed enhanced PVK films quality with decreased defects [3]. Moreover, the TRPL spectroscopy (Fig. 2j) directly derives the carrier lifetime of various glass/PVK samples through the exponential function:

The τ ave, τ 1 and τ 2 are obviously increased from 0.19, 0.15 and 4.20 ns for glass/CsPbBr3 film to 0.45, 0.29 and 12.50 ns for A/I Br-CQDs tailored sample (Table S2), respectively, demonstrating that the carrier in the latter film has a longer lifetime to be extracted by the CTLs to improve the current output, cross-validating the results of PL analysis and the fewer grain boundaries as well as defects in optimized PVK film.

Subsequently, the fabricated PVK films were used as the light-absorbing layers to construct all-inorganic HTMs-free PSCs, Fig. 3a-c display the cross-sectional SEM images of various devices. The thickness of Br-CQDs modification on c-TiO2/m-TiO2 film (Fig. 3c) is not visibly observed due to the small size of Br-CQDs, and combined with the similar surface morphology of c-TiO2/m-TiO2 and c-TiO2/m-TiO2/Br-CQDs films shown in Fig. S7, indicating a super-thin thickness of the Br-CQDs interfacial layer, which facilitates electron extraction and transfer. Notably, the slight difference in thickness of the three TiO2 ETLs is mainly owing to the mesoporous property of.

m-TiO2 layer, which results in uneven TiO2 layers with nonuniform thickness [37,38], also as evidenced by the different thickness of the entire cross-section in the three TiO2 ETLs. In addition, the cross-sectional morphological characteristics of PVK layer are remarkably different. For the control sample (Fig. 3a), the PVK layer consists of grains with different sizes and even multi-layer polycrystalline structure (such as the blue regions) owing to the speedy grain growth process, which implys that the photogenerated carriers would encounter excess grain boundaries during transfer process for rigorous carriers recombination inside the PVK bulk and at the PVK/CTLs interfaces [39]. After employing Br-CQDs as additive to effectively delay the grain crystallization and growth process, the light-absorbing layer (Fig. 3b) displays more regular cross-sectional morphology and the grains becomes more uniform, but there are still a few aggregated polycrystalline at the TiO2/PVK interface, such as the red regions. Meaningfully, by further depositing Br-CQDs on the TiO2 film surface, the resulted PVK layer (Fig. 3c) exhibits the behavior of vertical single-crystal distributions and the grains are most uniform and neatly arranged, which can almost be attributed to the fact that Br-CQDs deposited at TiO2 surface can further anchor the uncoordinated Pb2+ and Cs+ ions for promoting the in-plane vertical growth of PVK grains [40]. Such cross-sectional structural behavior is in favor of reducing series resistance as well as boosting charge extraction and transfer at the interface for suppressing charge recombination and energy loss. The confocal PL microscopy test on the cross sections of all PVK films were performed to explore charge recombination behaviour inside the various samples (Fig. 3d-e). Apparently, with the introducing of Br-CQDs as additive and further interface modifier, the luminescence becomes more and more continuous and brighter, especially the black gaps induced by carrier recombination in the yellow luminescence are constantly lit up, which cross-demonstrates the suppressed charge recombination inside the PVK block and/or at buried interface after incorporation of Br-CQDs owing to the reduced grain boundaries and ions defect within the light absorbing layer [39].

The as-prepared PVK film inevitably suffers from tensile strain to cause the formation of residual stress owing to its soft lattice properties and the difference in thermal expansion coefficients between PVK and substrate, which seriously damages the efficiency and long-term stability of PSCs [41,42]. Considering the insertion of Br-CQDs additive in PVK lattice interstices during CsPbBr3 growth process (Fig. 1e) and the anchor of bromine and oxygen-containing groups in Br-CQDs with Pb and Cs atoms (Fig. 1f) both inside the PVK bulk and at the TiO2/PVK interface, the thermal expansion of PVK lattice after incorporation of Br-CQDs would be impeded to decrease the residual stress. To deeply explore and highlight the effect of Br-CQDs modifier on the strain evolution of the PVK film, the GIXRD characterization was performed, as depicted in Fig. 3g-i. As the incident angle increases from 0.1° to 1°, the diffraction peak for the control PVK film shifts obviously to lower angles, which corresponds to continuously increasing interplanar distance, suggesting that the PVK film stays in a state of tensile strain. A larger tensile strain will cause more severe lattice distortion, higher barrier of charge transport and smaller activation energy of ions migration to descend the PSCs performance. By contrast, the Br-CQDs tailored PVK film exhibits a significantly weakened shift of the characteristic peaks. The linear relationship between displacing values for (100) plane and incident angle in Fig. 4i more directly illustrates the contracted lattices across entire PVK film especially in bottom area due to the lower thermal expansion coefficient of TiO2 in comparison with that of PVK, and the lattice strain release in Br-CQDs tailored sample. Because of the strong electron-withdrawing behavior of Br-CQDs to anchor the uncoordinated Pb2+ and Cs+ ions in PVK, the Br-CQDs modifier can bond with the PVK crystal like a lattice “adhesive” to restore the soft lattice of PVK and inhibit the lattice expansion, therefore releasing the tensile strain at buried interface and within PVK film, as shown in Fig. 3j.

Importantly, the introduction of Br-CQDs with excellent incident light transmittance (Fig. S8) can also regulate the band alignment at the PVK/CTLs interfaces. According to the UPS and Tauc plots of various PVK films and Br-CQDs layer presented in Fig. S9 and S10, respectively, the energy level diagram of the whole device is illustrated in Fig. 4a. Apparently, the E VB (−5.55 eV) of the Br-CQDs incorporated PVK film is obviously upshifted in comparison with − 5.60 eV of the control one, indicating a better-matched energy level gradient at the former PVK/Carbon interface. Moreover, the energy level barrier in the electron transfer process from PVK to TiO2 is also remarkably decreased via constructing the intermediate energy level of Br-CQDs with a CB of − 3.89 eV at the interface, which is conducive to the acceleration of charge extraction and transportation to suppress charge recombination.

Subsequently, the J-V curves of various PSCs are presented in Fig. 4b, Table 1 summarizes the specific photovoltaic parameters. The control PSC displays an inferior photovoltaic performance with a PCE of 8.47%, meanwhile the A Br-CQDs based device exhibits an improved PCE of 10.17%, in addition, with further introducing Br-CQDs at the interface, the corresponding PSCs performance is significantly boosted and all the parameters have a higher repeatability (Fig. S11), especially A/I Br-CQDs based PSC exhibits a champion PCE up to 10.79% (J SC: 7.84 mA cm−2, V OC: 1.651 V, FF: 83.36%), achieving an impressive enhancement compared to the efficiencies of A Br-CQDs based PSCs and other reported CsPbBr3 solar cells [8,11–13,20,43–52]. To the best of our knowledge, the PCE up to 10.79% and the V OC of 1.651 V are till now the highest value among the photovoltaic parameters of the mesoporous CsPbBr3 PSCs without HTMs (as summarized in Table S3). The evidently improved photovoltaic performance is on account of the markedly suppression of carrier recombination and energy loss due to the well-matched interfaces energy level as well as the higher-quality PVK film with reduced defect states, released residual stress, higher electron and hole mobility after incorporating Br-CQDs modifier. As the EQE spectra along with integrated current density shown in Fig. 4c, all PSCs show a superior spectral response under the wavelength range of 325–550 nm, which is completely in line with the UV–vis light absorption. The integrated current density is 6.76, 7.43 and 7.72 mA cm−2 for the control, A Br-CQDs and A/I Br-CQDs tailored PSCs, respectively, cross-validating the accuracy of results from J-V curves. As expected, all the Br-CQDs based PSCs display a higher and more stable photocurrent and PCE output in comparison with the control device (Fig. S12), which proves that introducing Br-CQDs into the PSCs can significantly enhance the output stability and photovoltaic performance. The hysteresis effect, an important parameter for evaluating device performance, of various PSCs is also shown in Fig. S13. The weaker hysteresis of A/I Br-CQDs modified device compared with other PSCs is almost ascribed to the reduced charge accumulation and energy loss at PVK/CTLs interface and the improved charge transport dynamics caused by the more matching interface energy level and higher-quality PVK film after introducing Br-CQDs as additive and interface modifier.

To further investigate the underlying mechanism behind performance enhancement of the Br-CQDs modified PSCs, the functional relationship of V OC and J SC between the incident-light intensity (I) are fitted in accordance with the following two formula: V OC= nkTln(I)/q + constant and J SC∝I α [51,53]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the control device has a maximum slope of 1.731 kT/q, and the slope value is markedly decreased to 1.665 kT/q and 1.619 kT/q for A Br-CQDs and A/I Br-CQDs tailored PSCs, respectively, indicating the effectively suppressed non-radiative recombination inside the optimized device. Meanwhile, the obviously increased α value of 0.994 for the optimized PSC compared to 0.958 for the control device in Fig. S14 demonstrates the remarkably reduced radiative recombination owing to the accelerated charge extraction and separation arising from the improved quality and charge mobility of PVK film and well-matched interface energy level after introducing A/I Br-CQDs. In order to better verify the accelerated charge extraction and separation inside the device, the hole and electron mobility (μ h and μ e) of the PSCs with different structure are ascertained from Fig. S15 according to the Mott-Gurney’s square law: μ h/μ e = 8J D L 3/9εε 0 V 2 [54], the specific values are summarized in Table S1. Apparently, the μ h and μ e are obviously boosted from 6.03 × 10−3 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 1.31 × 10−5 cm2 V−1 s−1 for the control one to 1.78 × 10−2 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 9.06 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1 for the A/I Br-CQDs tailored sample, respectively, which is attributed to the higher-quality and more matching interfaces energy level of the latter PVK film. In addition, compared to the control sample, the more dramatic PL quenching phenomenon (Fig. 4e) and the shorter carrier lifetime (Fig. 4f and Table S4) of A/I Br-CQDs modified sample indicate significant promotion of carrier separation and extraction, cross-validating the boosted charge migration behavior and the reduced PVK defect states, which helps to further improve the charge transfer dynamics and suppress charge recombination inside the device.

Fig. 4g shows the EIS to better explore the charge transfer dynamics inside the PSCs. From the equivalent circuit diagram inserted in Fig. 4g, R rec and R s are the charge recombination resistance and series resistance associating with the circular arc and the intersection of the real axis, respectively [41]. The higher R rec and the smaller R s of A/I Br-CQDs tailored PSCs compared to those of other devices suggest a decreased charge recombination rate and an accelerated charge transfer as a result of the more violent resistance during charge recombination and lessen resistance encountered in charge transfer process. The Mott-Schottky curves were also characterized to probe the charge transport and recombination behavior, as shown in Fig. 4h. The A/I Br-CQDs tailored PSCs exhibit a larger built-in potential (1.405 V), V bi, than 1.247 V and 1.381 V for the control and A Br-CQDs incorporated PSCs, respectively, illustrating that the former PSC can provide a stronger driving force to promote electron-hole pairs separation, extraction, transportation and simultaneously supply a longer depletion region to retard charge recombination for obtaining a higher J SC and V OC output. Furthermore, the lower dark current density of A/I Br-CQDs tailored PSCs than that of other devices (Fig. 4i) repeatedly evidences the reduced current leakage paths (grain boundaries and defects) and the suppressed charge recombination of the former PSCs.

In addition to low production cost and high efficiency output, the outstanding long-term stability is equally important for achieving commercial application of PSCs. To confirm the enhanced tolerability of optimized PSC under persistent attacks by high temperature, high humidity and continuous illumination, the long-term stability of various PSCs without any encapsulation are detected in air. According to Fig. 5a and b, the optimal PSC exhibits a best superior long-term stability with 97% and 98% PCE retention in 85% RH, 85 °C environment over 1500 h and under continuous illumination over 800 h, respectively, which is remarkably higher than 88% and 85% for the control device, 93% and 91% for the A Br-CQDs based PSC, mainly attributing to the collaborative prevention of moisture invasion due to the higher-quality PVK film with fewer defect states and released lattice stress as well as carbon electrode with hydrophobic property [13]. Besides, the larger contact angles of A/I and A Br-CQDs modified PVK films in Fig. 5c further confirm the significantly improved hydrophobicity of PVK film with reduced grain boundary, which also plays a role in impeding humidity erosion.

As a summary, the multifunctional Br-CQDs with high healing effect and electronic conductivity are successfully synthesized and employed as additive and interface modifier to fabricate efficiency and stable all-inorganic carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs without HTMs. Due to the anchor of bromine and oxygen-containing groups in Br-CQDs with uncoordinated Pb2+ and Cs+ ions at surface and in bulk of PVK and the insertion of Br-CQDs additive in PVK lattice, the introduction of Br-CQDs like a lattice “adhesive” reduces the nucleation and growth, the trap states emergence and the thermal expansion of CsPbBr3 grains, resulting in the formation of a higher-quality PVK film with larger grain size, fewer defects and tensile strain, which boosts carrier transfer and separation and restrains carrier recombination. In addition, the use of Br-CQDs as interface modifier together with additive also regulates the device interfacial energy level to accelerate charge transport and extraction and minimize energy loss. Finally, the Br-CQDs tailored device with a construction of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/Br-CQDs/CsPbBr3 +Br-CQDs/Carbon achieves a maximum PCE up to 10.79%. Furthermore, the optimized PSC exhibits a more excellent long-term stability under 85% RH at 85 °C and continuous illumination without any encapsulation. All the analysis and results demonstrate that our work provides a novel and promising approach for achieving super-high efficiency and stable carbon-based all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs without HTMs, which is expected to realize the balance between high efficiency and poor stability to greatly promote commercialization process of PSCs.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para:
Image 1
Jingwei Zhu has been working toward the M.S. degree in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Ocean University of China (OUC) since 2018. His research interests are mainly focused on inorganic perovskite solar cells.

Image 2
Benlin He is an associate Professor in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Ocean University of China (OUC). After receiving her Ph.D. at Lanzhou University, P R China, she joined the School of Materials Science and Engineering at OUC in 2007. Her research interests cover inorganic perovskite solar cells, dye-sensitized solar cells, and multi-energy integrated solar cells.

Image 3
Mengxin Wang has been working toward the M.S. degree in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Ocean University of China (OUC) since 2017. Her research interests are mainly focused on inorganic perovskite solar cells.

Image 4
XinPeng Yao has been working toward the M.S. degree in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Ocean University of China (OUC) since 2019. His research interests are mainly focused on inorganic perovskite solar cells.

Image 5
Hao Huang is currently an assistant professor at the School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University. He received his BS and PhD degree from Wuhan University in 2016 and 2021, respectively. His research interest mainly focuses on nanoelectronics and nanophotonics.

Image 6
Cong Chen received his B.S. and Ph.D. degree from Wuhan University in 2016 and 2021, respectively. He studied in Prof. Yanfa Yan’s group as a visiting student at The University of Toledo from 2017 to 2020. Currently, He is an associate Professor in the School of Materials Science and Engineering of Sichuan University. His research interest mainly focuses on wide-bandgap perovskite solar cells and tandem solar cells.

Image 7
Haiyan Chen is a lecture in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Ocean University of China (OUC). After receiving her Ph.D. at Shandong University, PR China, she joined the School of Materials Science and Engineering at OUC in 2003 as a lecture. Her research interests cover inorganic perovskite solar cells and dye-sensitized solar cells.

Image 8
Yanyan Duan received her Ph.D. degree in Marinechemical Engineering and Technology at Ocean University of China in 2018. She is currently a lecture at School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University. Her research interests include all-inorganic perovskite solar cells, dye-sensitized solar cells, and multi-energy integrated solar cells.

Image 9
Qunwei Tang is a full Professor in the Institute of Carbon Neutrality, College of Chemical and Biological Engineering at Shandong University of Science and Technology. After receiving his Ph.D. at Huaqiao University, P R China, he joined Strasbourg University in France and the University of South Carolina in USA as a postdoctoral fellow. His research interests cover perovskite solar cells and solar-hydrogen production.
